rendered_brief:
OTIF ROOT-CAUSE BOARD BRIEF

Scope: 140 failed orders representing AED 1,398,773 in order value at risk.

RANKED DRIVERS BY CASH IMPACT

supplier — AED 551,206 (39% of total)
Supplier lateness clustered across inbound POs arriving two to twelve days late, leaving insufficient on-hand stock at pick time and directly causing quantity shortfalls. In contested cases where demand also fired, supplier lateness dominated whenever the late PO volume alone could have closed the gap or when the forecast miss was insufficient to explain the full shortfall unaided. Action: implement a supplier on-time delivery scorecard with contractual lead-time penalties for the highest-frequency offenders, and require advance-ship notices to trigger early escalation when a PO is at risk of missing its due date.

demand — AED 523,886 (37% of total)
Demand under-forecasting drove stock-outs at pick across a large number of orders, with forecast errors ranging from roughly twenty percent to over fifty percent; in the majority of pure-demand cases the forecast miss equalled or exceeded the shortfall, confirming under-forecasting as the binding cause. Action: deploy a statistical safety-stock uplift calibrated to observed forecast-error volatility by SKU, and introduce a rolling forecast-accuracy review cadence to identify and retrain the worst-performing SKU families.

warehouse — AED 200,268 (14% of total)
Warehouse failures split into two modes: dispatch delays of one to six days that alone pushed delivery past the promised date with no pick short or upstream cause, and short picks where stock was physically sufficient at pick time yet units were not fulfilled. Action: set a same-day dispatch SLA for all orders with a promised date within the standard transit window, and introduce a cycle-count trigger whenever a pick-short event occurs against a location showing sufficient on-hand inventory.

logistics — AED 123,414 (9% of total)
Transit overages were the sole driver for logistics-attributed orders; in every pure-logistics case no dispatch delay or other domain fired, and the transit overage alone pushed delivery past the promised date. Action: renegotiate carrier SLAs on the highest-overage lanes and introduce real-time shipment-tracking alerts to enable proactive customer communication when a transit breach is detected mid-journey.

WHAT COULD NOT BE ESTABLISHED
For the subset of orders where both demand and supplier signals fired simultaneously, the investigation could not determine with certainty the precise sequence of stock depletion versus replenishment failure; the attribution reflects the dominant signal based on whether the forecast miss or the late PO volume was sufficient alone to explain the shortfall, but joint-cause orders may warrant a deeper time-series stock reconstruction to confirm. Additionally, the root cause of warehouse short-pick events — where stock was physically present yet not fulfilled — could not be established from the available signals; possible explanations include location-level inventory discrepancies, pick-path errors, or system reservation conflicts, none of which are captured in the current signal set.
